title: Mechanotransduction

infobox.value: Integrins, cytoskeleton | Stress fiber formation, contractility

(*[mechano](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mechano-#Prefix "wikt:mechano-")* + *[transduction](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/transduction#Noun "wikt:transduction")*) is any of various [mechanisms](/wiki/Mechanism_(biology) "Mechanism (biology)") by which [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") convert [mechanical stimulus](/wiki/Wolff%27s_law "Wolff's law") into a nerve signal.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) This form of [sensory transduction](/wiki/Sensory_transduction "Sensory transduction") is responsible for a number of [senses](/wiki/Sense "Sense") and [physiological](/wiki/Physiology "Physiology") processes in the body, including [proprioception](/wiki/Proprioception "Proprioception"), [touch](/wiki/Somatosensory_system "Somatosensory system"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) [balance](/wiki/Balance_(ability) "Balance (ability)"), and [hearing](/wiki/Hearing "Hearing").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The basic mechanism of mechanotransduction involves [converting mechanical signals into electrical or chemical signals](/wiki/Signal_transduction "Signal transduction").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Conversion of mechanical stimulus of a cell into electrochemical activity

See also: [Mechanosensation](/wiki/Mechanosensation "Mechanosensation")

In [cellular biology](/wiki/Cellular_biology "Cellular biology"), **mechanotransduction** (*[mechano](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mechano-#Prefix "wikt:mechano-")* + *[transduction](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/transduction#Noun "wikt:transduction")*) is any of various [mechanisms](/wiki/Mechanism_(biology) "Mechanism (biology)") by which [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") convert [mechanical stimulus](/wiki/Wolff%27s_law "Wolff's law") into a nerve signal.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) This form of [sensory transduction](/wiki/Sensory_transduction "Sensory transduction") is responsible for a number of [senses](/wiki/Sense "Sense") and [physiological](/wiki/Physiology "Physiology") processes in the body, including [proprioception](/wiki/Proprioception "Proprioception"), [touch](/wiki/Somatosensory_system "Somatosensory system"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) [balance](/wiki/Balance_(ability) "Balance (ability)"), and [hearing](/wiki/Hearing "Hearing").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The basic mechanism of mechanotransduction involves [converting mechanical signals into electrical or chemical signals](/wiki/Signal_transduction "Signal transduction").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Molecular_Machines_of_Life.jpg/250px-Molecular_Machines_of_Life.jpg)](/wiki/File:Molecular_Machines_of_Life.jpg)

Some [biological machines](/wiki/Biological_machine "Biological machine")

In this process, a mechanically gated ion channel makes it possible for sound, pressure, or movement to cause a change in the excitability of specialized sensory cells and [sensory neurons](/wiki/Sensory_neuron "Sensory neuron").[[9]](#cite_note-9) The stimulation of a [mechanoreceptor](/wiki/Mechanoreceptor "Mechanoreceptor") causes mechanically sensitive [ion channels](/wiki/Ion_channels "Ion channels") to open and produce a transduction current that changes the membrane potential of the cell.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Typically the mechanical stimulus gets filtered in the conveying medium before reaching the site of mechanotransduction.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Cellular responses to mechanotransduction are variable and give rise to a variety of changes and sensations. Broader issues involved include molecular [biomechanics](/wiki/Biomechanics "Biomechanics").

Single-molecule biomechanics studies of proteins and DNA, and mechanochemical coupling in [molecular motors](/wiki/Molecular_motors "Molecular motors") have demonstrated the critical importance of molecular mechanics as a new frontier in [bioengineering](/wiki/Bioengineering "Bioengineering") and life sciences. Current findings indicate that the mechanotransduction channel in hair cells complex [biological machine](/wiki/Biological_machine "Biological machine"). Mechanotransduction also includes the use of chemical energy to do mechanical work.[[12]](#cite_note-NakanoEckford2013-12)

## Cellular mechanotransduction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mechanotransduction&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Cellular mechanotransduction")]

Cells continuously detect and respond to mechanical cues in their individual environments: substrate stiffness, shear stress, tension, and compression. These forces are converted into biochemical signals through mechanotransduction pathways, allowing cells to regulate their behavior: migration, death, structural changes, gene expression, and [cell differentiation](/wiki/Cellular_differentiation "Cellular differentiation").[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13)

### General cellular mechanotransduction pathway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mechanotransduction&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: General cellular mechanotransduction pathway")]

The following are the typical steps of a mechanotransduction pathway:[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13)

1. A mechanical force is applied (tension, rigidity, friction, sheer force, etc).
2. A force-sensitive protein (often [mechanosensitive channels](/wiki/Mechanosensitive_channels "Mechanosensitive channels")) responds to the mechanical force.
3. An electrochemical gradient or change in cell polarity is produced directly or indirectly by the force-sensitive protein.
4. This gradient or polarity produces an effect or output that changes cell behavior.

### Mechanical sensing structures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mechanotransduction&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mechanical sensing structures")]

Cells use the following structures in specialized ways to detect mechanical forces.

* [Ion channels](/wiki/Ion_channel "Ion channel") - [mechanosensitive channels](/wiki/Mechanosensitive_channels "Mechanosensitive channels") modulate their activity based on physical pressures.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* [Extracellular matrix](/wiki/Extracellular_matrix "Extracellular matrix") linked proteins - [integrins](/wiki/Integrin "Integrin") and [focal adhesions](/wiki/Focal_adhesion "Focal adhesion") can transmit forces from the linked [extracellular matrix](/wiki/Extracellular_matrix "Extracellular matrix") to the [cytoskeleton](/wiki/Cytoskeleton "Cytoskeleton").[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13)
* [Cytoskeleton](/wiki/Cytoskeleton "Cytoskeleton") - [actin](/wiki/Actin "Actin"), [microtubules](/wiki/Microtubule "Microtubule"), and [intermediate filaments](/wiki/Intermediate_filament "Intermediate filament") act as force-transmitting networks.[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13)
* Nucleus - deformations of the [nuclear lamina](/wiki/Nuclear_lamina "Nuclear lamina") can influence gene expression.[[15]](#cite_note-:3-15)

Mechanical forces applied to these structures can activate intracellular signaling pathways, alter cytoskeletal tension, and change gene transcription (see linked sources for specific pathways).

Examples of these mechanical sensing structures leading to mechanotransduced behavior changes are listed below.

Cellular Mechanotransduction Pathway Examples

| Mechanical Cue | Cellular Sensor | Common Cellular Response(s) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Substrate stiffness | Integrins, cytoskeleton, nucleus | Differentiation,[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) [apoptosis](/wiki/Apoptosis "Apoptosis"),[[15]](#cite_note-:3-15) migration[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) |
| Sheer stress | Ion channels, [glycocalyx](/wiki/Glycocalyx "Glycocalyx") | Endothelial alignment[[16]](#cite_note-16) |
| Compression | Cytoskeleton, nucleus | Gene regulation[[15]](#cite_note-:3-15) |
| Tension | Integrins, cytoskeleton | Stress fiber formation, contractility |

## Aberrant cellular mechanotransduction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mechanotransduction&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Aberrant cellular mechanotransduction")]

Unusual or altered mechanotransduction has been correlated with changes in cell behavior. As well, changes to the medium on which cells grow (the extracellular matrix) can often regulate cell activity through to mechanotransduction. Thus, these changes to the extracellular matrix can result in unhealthy cell activity.

### Cancer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mechanotransduction&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Cancer")]

Cell behavior changes resulting from aberrant mechanotransduction may lead to cancer. Substrate stiffness (more abundant around cancerous tissue) can be transduced into signaling pathways that lead to cell death or migration.[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13)[[15]](#cite_note-:3-15) [Apoptosis](/wiki/Apoptosis "Apoptosis") can be regulated by the stiffness of the extracellular matrix.[[15]](#cite_note-:3-15) Rigidity-sensing complexes regulating where cells grow or travel is partially managed by mechanotransduction.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17) Alterations to this rigidity-sensing mechanotransduction can promote cancer: loss in rigidity-sensing complexes can, for example, lead to growth on soft tissue without apoptosis.[[18]](#cite_note-:0-18)

Cross-linked extracellular matrix proteins in tumors leads to stiffness that has been shown to favor cancer development.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Through mechano-sensitive pathways, this stiffness can lead to metastasis through the stimulation of [epithelial-mesenchymal transition](/wiki/Epithelial%E2%80%93mesenchymal_transition "Epithelial–mesenchymal transition") (EMT)[[20]](#cite_note-20) which is associated with [anoikis](/wiki/Anoikis "Anoikis") avoidance - stimulating cell migration.

### Fibrosis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mechanotransduction&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Fibrosis")]

Mechanotransduction pathways, such as FAK-ERK, have been shown to be involved in fibrosis.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Ear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mechanotransduction&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Ear")]

Air pressure changes in the ear canal cause the vibrations of the [tympanic membrane](/wiki/Tympanic_membrane "Tympanic membrane") and middle ear [ossicles](/wiki/Ossicles "Ossicles"). At the end of the ossicular chain, movement of the [stapes](/wiki/Stapes "Stapes") footplate within the [oval window](/wiki/Oval_window "Oval window") of the cochlea generates a pressure field within the cochlear fluids, imparting a pressure differential across the [basilar membrane](/wiki/Basilar_membrane "Basilar membrane"). A sinusoidal pressure wave results in localized vibrations of the [organ of Corti](/wiki/Organ_of_Corti "Organ of Corti"): near the base for high frequencies, near the apex for low frequencies.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Hair cells in the cochlea are stimulated when the basilar membrane is driven up and down by differences in the fluid pressure between the [scala vestibuli](/wiki/Scala_vestibuli "Scala vestibuli") and [scala tympani](/wiki/Scala_tympani "Scala tympani"). This motion is accompanied by a shearing motion between the [tectorial membrane](/wiki/Tectorial_membrane_(cochlea) "Tectorial membrane (cochlea)") and the [reticular lamina](/wiki/Reticular_lamina "Reticular lamina") of the organ of Corti, causing the hair bundles that link the two to be deflected, initiating mechano-electrical transduction. When the basilar membrane is driven upward, shear between the hair cells and the tectorial membrane deflects hair bundles in the excitatory direction, toward their tall edge. At the midpoint of an oscillation the hair bundles resume their resting position. When the basilar membrane moves downward, the hair bundles are driven in the inhibitory direction.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Skeletal muscle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mechanotransduction&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Skeletal muscle")]

When a deformation is imposed on a muscle, changes in cellular and molecular conformations link the mechanical forces with biochemical signals, and the close integration of mechanical signals with electrical, metabolic, and hormonal signaling may disguise the aspect of the response that is specific to the mechanical forces.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Cartilage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mechanotransduction&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Cartilage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Mechanically_Gated_Channel.png/250px-Mechanically_Gated_Channel.png)](/wiki/File:Mechanically_Gated_Channel.png)

Mechanically gated channel

One of the main mechanical functions of articular [cartilage](/wiki/Cartilage "Cartilage") is to act as a low-friction, load-bearing surface. Due to its unique location at joint surfaces, articular cartilage experiences a range of static and dynamic forces that include shear, compression and tension. These mechanical loads are absorbed by the cartilage extracellular matrix, where they are subsequently dissipated and transmitted to [chondrocytes](/wiki/Chondrocytes "Chondrocytes") (cartilage cells).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Cartilage_forces.jpg/250px-Cartilage_forces.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cartilage_forces.jpg)

Cartilage experience tension, compression and shear forces in vivo

Chondrocytes sense and convert the mechanical signals they receive into biochemical signals, which subsequently direct and mediate both [anabolic](/wiki/Anabolic "Anabolic") (matrix building) and [catabolic](/wiki/Catabolic "Catabolic") (matrix degrading) processes. These processes include the synthesis of matrix proteins ([type II collagen](/wiki/Type_II_collagen "Type II collagen") and [proteoglycans](/wiki/Proteoglycans "Proteoglycans")), [proteases](/wiki/Proteases "Proteases"), protease inhibitors, [transcription factors](/wiki/Transcription_factors "Transcription factors"), [cytokines](/wiki/Cytokines "Cytokines") and [growth factors](/wiki/Growth_factors "Growth factors").[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

The balance that is struck between [anabolic](/wiki/Anabolic "Anabolic") and [catabolic](/wiki/Catabolic "Catabolic") processes is strongly influenced by the type of loading that cartilage experiences. High strain rates (such as which occurs during impact loading) cause tissue damage, degradation, decreased matrix production and [apoptosis](/wiki/Apoptosis "Apoptosis").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) Decreased mechanical loading over long periods, such as during extended bed-rest, causes a loss of matrix production.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Static loads have been shown to be detrimental to biosynthesis[[30]](#cite_note-30) while oscillatory loads at low frequencies (similar that of a normal walking gait) have been shown to be beneficial in maintaining health and increasing matrix synthesis.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Due to the complexity of in-vivo loading conditions and the interplay of other mechanical and biochemical factors, the question of what an optimal loading regimen may be or whether one exists remain unanswered.

Although studies have shown that, like most biological tissues, cartilage is capable of mechanotransduction, the precise mechanisms by which this is done remain unknown. However, there exist a few hypotheses which begin with the identification of [mechanoreceptors](/wiki/Mechanoreceptors "Mechanoreceptors").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In order for mechanical signals to be sensed, there need to be mechanoreceptors on the surface of chondrocytes. Candidates for chondrocyte mechanoreceptors include [stretch-activated ion channels](/wiki/Stretch-activated_ion_channel "Stretch-activated ion channel") (SAC),[[32]](#cite_note-32) the [hyaluronan](/wiki/Hyaluronan "Hyaluronan") receptor [CD44](/wiki/CD44 "CD44"), [annexin V](/wiki/Annexin_V "Annexin V") (a collagen type II receptor),[[33]](#cite_note-33) and [integrin](/wiki/Integrin "Integrin") receptors (of which there exist several types on chondrocytes).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Chondrocyte_receptors.jpg/250px-Chondrocyte_receptors.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chondrocyte_receptors.jpg)

Chondrocyte surface mechano-receptors include CD44, annexin V and integrins. Chondrocyte extracellular matrix components include collagens, proteoglycans (which consist of aggrecan and hyaluronan), fibronectin and COMP.

Using the integrin-linked mechanotransduction pathway as an example (being one of the better studied pathways), it has been shown to mediate chondrocyte adhesion to cartilage surfaces,[[34]](#cite_note-34) mediate survival signaling[[35]](#cite_note-35) and regulate matrix production and degradation.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Integrin receptors have an extracellular domain that binds to the extracellular matrix proteins (collagen, [fibronectin](/wiki/Fibronectin "Fibronectin"), [laminin](/wiki/Laminin "Laminin"), [vitronectin](/wiki/Vitronectin "Vitronectin") and [osteopontin](/wiki/Osteopontin "Osteopontin")), and a cytoplasmic domain that interacts with intracellular signaling molecules. When an integrin receptor binds to its extracellular matrix ligand and is activated, additional integrins cluster around the activated site. In addition, [kinases](/wiki/Kinases "Kinases") (e.g., [focal adhesion kinase](/wiki/Focal_adhesion_kinase "Focal adhesion kinase") and [adapter proteins](/wiki/Signal_transducing_adaptor_protein "Signal transducing adaptor protein") (e.g., [paxillin](/wiki/Paxillin "Paxillin"), aka Pax, [talin](/wiki/Talin_protein "Talin protein"), aka Tal, and [Shc](/wiki/Src_homology_2_domain-containing "Src homology 2 domain-containing")) are recruited to this cluster, which is called the [focal adhesion](/wiki/Focal_adhesion "Focal adhesion") complex. The activation of these focal adhesion complex molecules in turn, triggers downstream events that up-regulate and/or down-regulate intracellular processes such as transcription factor activation and gene regulation resulting in apoptosis or differentiation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In addition to binding to extracellular matrix ligands, integrins are also receptive to [autocrine](/wiki/Autocrine "Autocrine") and [paracrine](/wiki/Paracrine "Paracrine") signals such as growth factors in the [TGF-beta](/wiki/TGF-beta "TGF-beta") family. Chondrocytes have been shown to secrete TGF-b, and upregulate TGF-b receptors in response to mechanical stimulation; this secretion may be a mechanism for autocrine signal amplification within the tissue.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Integrin signaling is just one example of multiple pathways that are activated when cartilage is loaded. Some intracellular processes that have been observed to occur within these pathways include phosphorylation of ERK1/2, p38 MAPK, and SAPK/ERK kinase-1 (SEK-1) of the JNK pathway[[38]](#cite_note-38) as well as changes in cAMP levels, actin re-organization and changes in the expression of genes which regulate cartilage extracellular matrix content.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

More recent studies have hypothesized that chondrocyte primary [cilium](/wiki/Cilium "Cilium") act as a mechanoreceptor for the cell, transducing forces from the extracellular matrix into the cell. Each chondrocyte has one cilium and it is hypothesized to transmit mechanical signals by way of bending in response to extracellular matrix loading. Integrins have been identified on the upper shaft of the cilium, acting as anchors to the collagen matrix around it.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Recent studies published by Wann *et al.* in FASEB Journal have demonstrated for the first time that primary cilia are required for chondrocyte mechanotransduction. Chondrocytes derived from IFT88 mutant mice did not express primary cilia and did not show the characteristic mechanosensitive up regulation of proteoglycan synthesis seen in wild type cells[[41]](#cite_note-41)

It is important to examine the mechanotransduction pathways in chondrocytes since mechanical loading conditions which represent an excessive or injurious response upregulates synthetic activity and increases catabolic signalling cascades involving mediators such as NO and MMPs. In addition, studies by Chowdhury TT and Agarwal S have shown that mechanical loading which represents physiological loading conditions will block the production of catabolic mediators (iNOS, COX-2, NO, PGE2) induced by inflammatory cytokines (IL-1) and restore anabolic activities. Thus an improved understanding of the interplay of biomechanics and cell signalling will help to develop therapeutic methods for blocking catabolic components of the mechanotransduction pathway. A better understanding of the optimal levels of in vivo mechanical forces are therefore necessary for maintaining the health and viability of cartilage, preventative techniques may be devised for the prevention of cartilage degradation and disease.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
